=== PLAN ===
# COR24 Ecosystem Refactoring Saga

Migrate ~19 repos across 3 GitHub orgs into canonical sw-cor24-* names
under sw-embed. Strategy: rename/fork/clone+push, preserve history,
deprecate old repos, build portal site.

See docs/refactoring-plan.md for full details.

=== STEPS ===
  [x] 001-trim-emulator [legacy]: Trim sw-cor24-emulator to its scope. Work in ~/github/sw-embed/sw-cor24-emula...
  [x] 002-trim-assembler [legacy]: Trim sw-cor24-assembler to assembler scope. Remove emulator internals, web app, components. Keep src/assembler.rs + tests. Add path dep on ../sw-cor24-emulator. Add scripts/build.sh. Ensure tests pass.
  [x] 003-trim-web-assembler [legacy]: Trim web-sw-cor24-assembler to web app scope. Remove CLI, rust-to-cor24, emulator/assembler source. Keep src/app.rs, components/, src/wasm.rs, src/challenge.rs, styles/, index.html, Trunk.toml. Update Cargo.toml path deps to ../sw-cor24-assembler and ../sw-cor24-emulator. Update Trunk.toml public_url. Add scripts/build-pages.sh and scripts/serve.sh. Verify trunk build works.
  [ ] 004-trim-rust [legacy]: Trim sw-cor24-rust to rust-to-cor24/ contents. Remove everything else. Restructure as top-level crate. Add path deps on ../sw-cor24-assembler and ../sw-cor24-emulator. Mark wasm2cor24 as deprecated. Add scripts/build.sh. <-- YOU ARE HERE
  [ ] 005-fork-pcode [legacy]: Fork pv24a to sw-cor24-pcode under sw-embed. Move contents into vm/ subdir. Copy pa24r into assembler/ and pl24r into linker/. Set up Rust workspace. Add scripts/build.sh. Ensure all tests pass.
  [ ] 006-fork-web-pcode [legacy]: Fork web-dv24r to web-sw-cor24-pcode under sw-embed. Update path deps to ../sw-cor24-pcode, ../sw-cor24-assembler, ../sw-cor24-emulator. Update Trunk.toml public_url. Add scripts/build-pages.sh and scripts/serve.sh.
  [ ] 007-fork-pascal [legacy]: Fork p24p to sw-cor24-pascal under sw-embed. Move contents into compiler/. Copy p24c into compiler-v2/ and pr24p into runtime/. Update paths to reference sw-cor24-pcode. Add scripts/build.sh. Ensure tests pass.
  [ ] 008-fork-web-pascal [legacy]: Fork web-p24c to web-sw-cor24-pascal under sw-embed. Update path deps. Update Trunk.toml public_url. Add scripts/build-pages.sh and scripts/serve.sh.
  [ ] 009-fork-tinyc [legacy]: Fork tc24r to sw-cor24-tinyc under sw-embed. Update path deps to ../sw-cor24-assembler and ../sw-cor24-emulator. Add scripts/build.sh.
  [ ] 010-fork-web-tinyc [legacy]: Fork web-tc24r to web-sw-cor24-tinyc under sw-embed. Update path deps and Trunk.toml. Add scripts.
  [ ] 011-fork-forth [legacy]: Fork tf24a to sw-cor24-forth under sw-embed. Update path deps. Add scripts/build.sh.
  [ ] 012-fork-web-forth [legacy]: Fork web-tf24a to web-sw-cor24-forth under sw-embed. Update path deps and Trunk.toml. Add scripts.
  [ ] 013-fork-macrolisp [legacy]: Fork tml24c to sw-cor24-macrolisp under sw-embed. Update path deps. Add scripts/build.sh.
  [ ] 014-fork-web-macrolisp [legacy]: Fork web-tml24c to web-sw-cor24-macrolisp under sw-embed. Update path deps and Trunk.toml. Add scripts.
  [ ] 015-fork-fortran [legacy]: Fork f24a to sw-cor24-fortran under sw-embed. Add scripts/build.sh.
  [ ] 016-fork-apl [legacy]: Fork a24a to sw-cor24-apl under sw-embed. Add scripts/build.sh.
  [ ] 017-deprecate-old-repos [legacy]: Add deprecation notices to all old repo READMEs. Link to new canonical repos under sw-embed.
  [ ] 018-portal-site [legacy]: Build sw-cor24-project GitHub Pages site with ISA overview, tutorials, language references, architecture diagrams, and links to all web-sw-cor24-* live demos.
  [ ] 019-verify-sweep [legacy]: Final sweep — verify all repos build/test, all demos deploy, all cross-repo links updated, all Cargo.toml package names correct, migration-status.md marked complete.

=== CURRENT STEP: 004-trim-rust [legacy] ===
Status: pending
Description: Trim sw-cor24-rust to rust-to-cor24/ contents. Remove everything else. Restructure as top-level crate. Add path deps on ../sw-cor24-assembler and ../sw-cor24-emulator. Mark wasm2cor24 as deprecated. Add scripts/build.sh.

=== WHEN DONE ===
Run: agentrail begin    (if not yet started)
Run: agentrail complete --summary "what you did" --next-slug <slug> --next-prompt "next instructions"
  Or: agentrail complete --summary "what you did" --done   (if this is the last step)

[workspace]
members = [".", "components", "cli", "isa"]
resolver = "2"

[package]
name = "cor24-emulator"
version = "0.1.0"
edition = "2024"
authors = ["Michael A Wright"]
license = "MIT"
description = "COR24 C-Oriented RISC 24-bit Assembly Emulator"

[lib]
crate-type = ["cdylib", "rlib"]

[features]
default = ["standalone"]
standalone = []

[dependencies]
cor24-isa = { path = "./isa", features = ["serde"] }
components = { path = "./components" }
yew = { version = "0.21", features = ["csr"] }
wasm-bindgen = "0.2"
serde = { version = "1.0", features = ["derive"] }
serde_json = "1.0"
serde-wasm-bindgen = "0.6"
web-sys = { version = "0.3", features = ["console"] }
gloo = "0.11"
getrandom = { version = "0.2", features = ["js"] }
console_error_panic_hook = "0.1"
thiserror = "2.0"

[profile.release]
opt-level = "z"
lto = true

[package]
name = "wasm2cor24"
version = "0.1.0"
edition = "2021"
description = "Translate WASM to COR24 assembly"
license = "MIT"
repository = "https://github.com/sw-embed/cor24-rs"

[workspace]
exclude = ["examples/blink", "demos/demo_echo"]

[dependencies]
wasmparser = "0.121"
anyhow = "1.0"
cor24-emulator = { path = ".." }
libc = "0.2"

[[bin]]
name = "wasm2cor24"
path = "src/main.rs"

[[bin]]
name = "msp430-to-cor24"
path = "src/msp430_cli.rs"

[[bin]]
name = "cor24-run"
path = "src/run.rs"

[dev-dependencies]

1	//! COR24 Assembler
2	//!
3	//! Parses COR24 assembly language and produces machine code.
4	//! Uses encoding tables extracted from the hardware decode ROM.
5	//!
6	//! COR24 is a C-Oriented RISC 24-bit architecture with:
7	//! - 3 general-purpose 24-bit registers (r0, r1, r2)
8	//! - 5 special registers: fp=r3, sp=r4, z=r5, iv=r6, ir=r7
9	//! - Single condition flag (C)
10	//! - Variable-length instructions (1, 2, or 4 bytes)
11	//! - 16MB address space (24-bit)
12	//! - Little-endian byte ordering

# sw-cor24-rust

Rust-to-COR24 translation pipeline. Compiles Rust programs to COR24
machine code via two translation paths:

- **wasm2cor24** (deprecated) -- Rust -> WASM -> COR24 assembly
- **msp430-to-cor24** -- Rust -> MSP430 assembly -> COR24 assembly
- **cor24-run** -- COR24 assembler + emulator CLI

## Dependencies

- [sw-cor24-emulator](https://github.com/sw-embed/sw-cor24-emulator) -- emulator + ISA
- [sw-cor24-assembler](https://github.com/sw-embed/sw-cor24-assembler) -- assembler

Both must be cloned as siblings under the same parent directory.

## Build

```bash
./scripts/build.sh
```

## Usage

```bash
# Translate MSP430 assembly to COR24
cargo run --bin msp430-to-cor24 -- input.s -o output.s

# Compile Rust project via MSP430 path
cargo run --bin msp430-to-cor24 -- --compile ./demos/demo_blinky

# Assemble and run COR24 assembly
cargo run --bin cor24-run -- --run program.s

# Run built-in demo
cargo run --bin cor24-run -- --demo
```

## Demos

The `demos/` directory contains small Rust programs targeting COR24 via
the MSP430 translation path. Each demo compiles to `#![no_std]` MSP430,
then translates to COR24 assembly.

1	# Rust to COR24 Pipeline
2	
3	Compiles Rust to COR24 machine code via the MSP430 target as a 16-bit intermediate.
4	
5	## Pipeline Overview
6	
7	```
8	                      ┌─────────┐
9	  demo_blinky.rs      │  rustc  │   Uses MSP430 as 16-bit backend
10	  (Rust source)  ───► │ nightly │   (no custom compiler needed)
11	                      │ msp430  │
12	                      └────┬────┘
13	                           │
14	                    ┌──────▼───────┐
15	                    │  .s file     │   MSP430 assembly text
16	                    │  (--emit asm)│   Functions in alphabetical section order
17	                    └──────┬───────┘
18	                           │
19	                 ┌─────────▼──────────┐
20	                 │  msp430-to-cor24   │   Translator (this project)
21	                 │  --entry demo_blinky│   Adds reset vector prologue
22	                 └─────────┬──────────┘
23	                           │
24	                    ┌──────▼───────┐
25	                    │  .cor24.s    │   COR24 assembly text
26	                    │              │   With `bra demo_blinky` at address 0
27	                    └──────┬───────┘
28	                           │
29	                    ┌──────▼───────┐
30	                    │  COR24       │   Two-pass assembler (in cor24-emulator crate)
31	                    │  Assembler   │   Resolves labels, encodes instructions
32	                    └──────┬───────┘
33	                           │
34	                    ┌──────▼───────┐
35	                    │  Binary blob │   Raw bytes, loaded at address 0x000000
36	                    └──────┬───────┘
37	                           │
38	                    ┌──────▼───────┐
39	                    │  COR24 CPU   │   PC starts at 0x000000 (RESET_ADDRESS)
40	                    │  Emulator    │   Executes `bra demo_blinky` first
41	                    └──────────────┘
42	```
43	
44	## How the Entry Point Works
45	
46	### The Problem
47	
48	The Rust compiler (`rustc`) emits functions in `.section .text.<name>` sections,
49	ordered **alphabetically by function name** — not by source order. So a file with
50	`demo_blinky`, `delay`, and `mmio_write` produces MSP430 assembly with sections in
51	this order:
52	
53	```
54	.section .text._RN...rust_begin_unwind   ← panic handler (first alphabetically!)
55	.section .text.delay
56	.section .text.demo_blinky               ← our entry point (buried in the middle)
57	.section .text.mmio_write
58	```
59	
60	The COR24 CPU starts executing at address 0x000000. Without intervention, it would
61	execute the panic handler (an infinite loop), not our demo.
62	
63	### The Solution: Reset Vector Prologue
64	
65	The `msp430-to-cor24` translator emits an **absolute jump at address 0** that
66	jumps to the `start` function. It uses `la`+`jmp` instead of `bra` because
67	`bra` has a ±127 byte offset limit:
68	
69	```asm
70	; Reset vector -> start
71	    la      r0, start            ; 4 bytes at address 0x000000
72	    jmp     (r0)                 ; 2 bytes at address 0x000004
73	
74	; --- function: _RN...rust_begin_unwind ---   (panic handler)
75	_RN...rust_begin_unwind:
76	    ...                          ; writes "PANIC\n" to UART, then infinite loop
77	
78	; --- function: demo_blinky ---
79	demo_blinky:
80	    la      r0, 0xFF0000
81	    lc      r1, 1
82	    ...
83	
84	; --- function: start ---                    ; ← CPU jumps here from address 0
85	start:
86	    jal     r1, demo_blinky      ; call the demo function
87	```
88	
89	This mirrors how real microcontrollers work: the hardware reset vector at address 0
90	contains a jump to the startup code.
91	
92	### The `start` Convention
93	
94	Every COR24 program compiled from Rust uses a fixed entry point convention:
95	
96	```rust
97	#[no_mangle]
98	pub unsafe fn start() -> ! {

# Rust to COR24 Pipeline

Compiles Rust to COR24 machine code via the MSP430 target as a 16-bit intermediate.

## Pipeline Overview

```
                      ┌─────────┐
  demo_blinky.rs      │  rustc  │   Uses MSP430 as 16-bit backend
  (Rust source)  ───► │ nightly │   (no custom compiler needed)
                      │ msp430  │
                      └────┬────┘
                           │
                    ┌──────▼───────┐
                    │  .s file     │   MSP430 assembly text
                    │  (--emit asm)│   Functions in alphabetical section order
                    └──────┬───────┘
                           │
                 ┌─────────▼──────────┐
                 │  msp430-to-cor24   │   Translator (this project)
                 │  --entry demo_blinky│   Adds reset vector prologue
                 └─────────┬──────────┘
                           │
                    ┌──────▼───────┐
                    │  .cor24.s    │   COR24 assembly text
                    │              │   With `bra demo_blinky` at address 0
                    └──────┬───────┘
                           │
                    ┌──────▼───────┐
                    │  COR24       │   Two-pass assembler (in cor24-emulator crate)
                    │  Assembler   │   Resolves labels, encodes instructions
                    └──────┬───────┘
                           │
                    ┌──────▼───────┐
                    │  Binary blob │   Raw bytes, loaded at address 0x000000
                    └──────┬───────┘
                           │
                    ┌──────▼───────┐
                    │  COR24 CPU   │   PC starts at 0x000000 (RESET_ADDRESS)
                    │  Emulator    │   Executes `bra demo_blinky` first
                    └──────────────┘
```

## How the Entry Point Works

### The Problem

The Rust compiler (`rustc`) emits functions in `.section .text.<name>` sections,
ordered **alphabetically by function name** — not by source order. So a file with
`demo_blinky`, `delay`, and `mmio_write` produces MSP430 assembly with sections in
this order:

```
.section .text._RN...rust_begin_unwind   ← panic handler (first alphabetically!)
.section .text.delay
.section .text.demo_blinky               ← our entry point (buried in the middle)
.section .text.mmio_write
```

The COR24 CPU starts executing at address 0x000000. Without intervention, it would
execute the panic handler (an infinite loop), not our demo.

### The Solution: Reset Vector Prologue

The `msp430-to-cor24` translator emits an **absolute jump at address 0** that
jumps to the `start` function. It uses `la`+`jmp` instead of `bra` because
`bra` has a ±127 byte offset limit:

```asm
; Reset vector -> start
    la      r0, start            ; 4 bytes at address 0x000000
    jmp     (r0)                 ; 2 bytes at address 0x000004

; --- function: _RN...rust_begin_unwind ---   (panic handler)
_RN...rust_begin_unwind:
    ...                          ; writes "PANIC\n" to UART, then infinite loop

; --- function: demo_blinky ---
demo_blinky:
    la      r0, 0xFF0000
    lc      r1, 1
    ...

; --- function: start ---                    ; ← CPU jumps here from address 0
start:
    jal     r1, demo_blinky      ; call the demo function
```

This mirrors how real microcontrollers work: the hardware reset vector at address 0
contains a jump to the startup code.

### The `start` Convention

Every COR24 program compiled from Rust uses a fixed entry point convention:

```rust
#[no_mangle]
pub unsafe fn start() -> ! {
    demo_blinky()       // calls the per-program named function
}
```

The `start()` function is the bridge between the CPU reset and the program logic.
Each program has exactly one `start()` that calls its main function.

### How the Entry Point is Identified

1. **Convention (default)**: The translator looks for a `start` label. If the input
   has `.globl` directives (i.e., comes from `rustc`) but no `start` label, it
   returns an error listing the available labels.

2. **Explicit `--entry <func>` flag** (optional override):
   ```bash
   msp430-to-cor24 input.msp430.s --entry my_entry -o output.cor24.s
   ```
   Only needed if a program can't use `start` for some reason.

3. **No `.globl` directives** (e.g., hand-written single-function tests):
   - No prologue emitted — first instruction is at address 0 (legacy behavior)

### In the Rust Source

```rust
#[no_mangle]
pub unsafe fn demo_blinky() -> ! {
    loop {
        mmio_write(LED_ADDR, 1);
        delay(5000);
        mmio_write(LED_ADDR, 0);
        delay(5000);
    }
}

#[no_mangle]
pub unsafe fn start() -> ! {
    demo_blinky()
}
```

The `#[no_mangle]` attribute makes both functions visible as `.globl` symbols in
the MSP430 assembly output. The translator finds `start` and emits the prologue.

## How to Run

### Quick start: run a pre-built demo

```bash
cd rust-to-cor24/demos
bash demo_fibonacci/run.sh
```

This does all three steps automatically:
1. Compiles `src/lib.rs` → `demo_fibonacci.msp430.s` (via `rustc`)
2. Translates → `demo_fibonacci.cor24.s` (via `msp430-to-cor24`)
3. Assembles + runs → dumps registers, memory, UART (via `cor24-run`)

### Run all 13 demos

```bash
cd rust-to-cor24/demos
bash generate-all.sh
```

### Step by step (manual)

```bash
# Build the tools first
cd rust-to-cor24
cargo build --release

# Step 1: Compile Rust → MSP430 assembly
cd demos/demo_fibonacci
rustup run nightly cargo rustc \
    --target msp430-none-elf \
    -Z build-std=core --release \
    -- --emit asm
cp target/msp430-none-elf/release/deps/demo_fibonacci*.s demo_fibonacci.msp430.s

# Step 2: Translate MSP430 → COR24 assembly
../../target/release/msp430-to-cor24 demo_fibonacci.msp430.s \
    -o demo_fibonacci.cor24.s --entry start

# Step 3: Assemble + run in COR24 emulator
../../target/release/cor24-run --run demo_fibonacci.cor24.s --dump
```

### Files produced

After running a demo, the directory contains:
```
demo_fibonacci/
    src/lib.rs                    ← Rust source (you write this)
    demo_fibonacci.msp430.s       ← MSP430 assembly (rustc output)
    demo_fibonacci.cor24.s        ← COR24 assembly (translator output)
    demo_fibonacci.log            ← Emulator output (registers, memory)
```

All intermediate files are human-readable text. There is no binary
object format — `cor24-run` assembles the `.cor24.s` file to bytes
in memory and executes directly.

## Register Mapping

| MSP430 | COR24 | Role |
|--------|-------|------|
| r12 | r0 | arg0 / return value |
| r13 | r1 | arg1 |
| r14 | r2 | arg2 |
| r1 | sp | stack pointer |
| r4-r11 | stack | spilled to fp-relative offsets |

## Calling Convention

The translator uses the `jal` (jump-and-link) calling convention, matching
MakerLisp's COR24 C compiler:

| Aspect | Implementation |
|--------|----------------|
| **Function call** | `la r2, func; jal r1,(r2)` — r1 gets return address |
| **Return** | `jmp (r1)` |
| **Arguments** | In r0 (from MSP430 r12 mapping) |
| **Return value** | In r0 |
| **Caller saves** | `push r1` before `jal`, `pop r1` after |

The `jal` instruction saves the return address in r1 and jumps to the
address in r2, in a single instruction.

## I/O Address Mapping

MSP430 uses 16-bit addresses. COR24 uses 24-bit. The translator maps:

| MSP430 (16-bit) | COR24 (24-bit) | Device |
|------------------|-----------------|--------|
| 0xFF00 | 0xFF0000 | LED D2 / Button S2 |
| 0xFF01 | 0xFF0100 | UART data register |
| 0xFF02 | 0xFF0101 | UART status register |

In Rust source, we define 16-bit constants that the MSP430 target can handle:
```rust
const LED_ADDR: u16 = 0xFF00;     // → 0xFF0000 after translation
const UART_DATA: u16 = 0xFF01;    // → 0xFF0100 after translation
```

## CLI Usage

```bash
# Translate MSP430 .s to COR24 assembly (uses start convention by default)
msp430-to-cor24 input.msp430.s -o output.cor24.s

# Override entry point (rare — only if start can't be used)
msp430-to-cor24 input.msp430.s --entry my_entry -o output.cor24.s

# Compile a Rust project end-to-end
msp430-to-cor24 --compile path/to/rust/project

# Run built-in test case
msp430-to-cor24 --test
```

### Compile mode prerequisites

```bash
rustup toolchain install nightly
rustup target add msp430-none-elf --toolchain nightly
```

## File Types in the Pipeline

| Extension | Format | Description |
|-----------|--------|-------------|
| `.rs` | Rust source | `#![no_std]`, `#[panic_handler]`, `#[no_mangle]` on entry |
| `.msp430.s` | MSP430 asm text | Output of `rustc --emit asm`, `.section .text.<func>` per function |
| `.cor24.s` | COR24 asm text | `la+jmp start` prologue + translated instructions + labels |
| (in-memory) | `Vec<u8>` | Raw bytes from COR24 assembler, loaded at address 0 |

## Project Structure

```
rust-to-cor24/
├── src/
│   ├── lib.rs           # Re-exports translate_msp430
│   ├── msp430.rs        # MSP430 parser + COR24 translator + entry point detection
│   ├── msp430_cli.rs    # CLI: --entry, -o, --compile, --test
│   ├── run.rs           # COR24 emulator runner (assembles + executes)
│   └── pipeline.rs      # WASM pipeline (legacy, unused)
├── output/              # Pre-generated demo outputs
│   ├── demo_blinky.msp430.s   # MSP430 asm from rustc
│   ├── demo_blinky.cor24.s    # COR24 asm with bra prologue
│   └── demo_blinky.log        # Emulator run output
└── data/
    └── pipeline/
        └── demos.rs     # Complete Rust source for all demos
```

## Conventions

1. **`start` entry point**: Every program has `#[no_mangle] pub unsafe fn start()` that calls the main function
2. **`#[no_mangle]`**: Required on `start`, entry functions, and all functions called across translation units
3. **`#[inline(never)]`**: Required on helper functions to prevent inlining (which would eliminate the callable function)
4. **`#![no_std]` + `#[panic_handler]`**: Required — no standard library on bare metal
5. **Panic handler**: Writes "PANIC\n" to UART before entering infinite loop
6. **Reset vector**: `la r0, start` + `jmp (r0)` at address 0 — the COR24 equivalent of a hardware reset vector
7. **Halt convention**: `loop {}` in Rust compiles to a self-branch (`bra .`), detected by the emulator as halt

# sw-cor24-rust

Rust-to-COR24 translation pipeline. Compiles Rust programs to COR24
machine code via the MSP430 target as a 16-bit intermediate.

## Pipeline

```
Rust source -> rustc (MSP430 target) -> MSP430 asm -> msp430-to-cor24 -> COR24 asm -> assembler -> binary -> emulator
```

## Binaries

- **msp430-to-cor24** -- MSP430 assembly to COR24 translator (primary path)
- **cor24-run** -- COR24 assembler + emulator CLI
- **wasm2cor24** -- WASM to COR24 translator (deprecated, MSP430 path preferred)

## Dependencies

Sibling repos (must be cloned alongside this repo):

- [sw-cor24-emulator](https://github.com/sw-embed/sw-cor24-emulator) -- emulator + ISA
- [sw-cor24-assembler](https://github.com/sw-embed/sw-cor24-assembler) -- assembler

## Build

```bash
./scripts/build.sh
```

## Usage

```bash
# Translate MSP430 assembly to COR24
cargo run --bin msp430-to-cor24 -- input.s -o output.s

# Compile Rust project end-to-end
cargo run --bin msp430-to-cor24 -- --compile ./demos/demo_blinky

# Assemble and run COR24 assembly
cargo run --bin cor24-run -- --run program.s

# Run built-in demo
cargo run --bin cor24-run -- --demo
```

## Demos

The `demos/` directory contains small `#![no_std]` Rust programs targeting
COR24 via the MSP430 translation path. See `demos/generate-all.sh` to build
all demos.

## Register Mapping

| MSP430 | COR24 | Role |
|--------|-------|------|
| r12 | r0 | arg0 / return value |
| r13 | r1 | arg1 |
| r14 | r2 | arg2 |
| r1 | sp | stack pointer |
| r4-r11 | stack | spilled to fp-relative offsets |

## Entry Point Convention

Every program needs `#[no_mangle] pub unsafe fn start()` as its entry
point. The translator emits a reset vector (`la r0, start` + `jmp (r0)`)
at address 0 to jump to `start`.

# Rust to COR24 Pipeline

Compiles Rust to COR24 machine code via the MSP430 target as a 16-bit intermediate.

## Pipeline Overview

```
                      ┌─────────┐
  demo_blinky.rs      │  rustc  │   Uses MSP430 as 16-bit backend
  (Rust source)  ───► │ nightly │   (no custom compiler needed)
                      │ msp430  │
                      └────┬────┘
                           │
                    ┌──────▼───────┐
                    │  .s file     │   MSP430 assembly text
                    │  (--emit asm)│   Functions in alphabetical section order
                    └──────┬───────┘
                           │
                 ┌─────────▼──────────┐
                 │  msp430-to-cor24   │   Translator (this project)
                 │  --entry demo_blinky│   Adds reset vector prologue
                 └─────────┬──────────┘
                           │
                    ┌──────▼───────┐
                    │  .cor24.s    │   COR24 assembly text
                    │              │   With `bra demo_blinky` at address 0
                    └──────┬───────┘
                           │
                    ┌──────▼───────┐
                    │  COR24       │   Two-pass assembler (in cor24-emulator crate)
                    │  Assembler   │   Resolves labels, encodes instructions
                    └──────┬───────┘
                           │
                    ┌──────▼───────┐
                    │  Binary blob │   Raw bytes, loaded at address 0x000000
                    └──────┬───────┘
                           │
                    ┌──────▼───────┐
                    │  COR24 CPU   │   PC starts at 0x000000 (RESET_ADDRESS)
                    │  Emulator    │   Executes `bra demo_blinky` first
                    └──────────────┘
```

## How the Entry Point Works

### The Problem

The Rust compiler (`rustc`) emits functions in `.section .text.<name>` sections,
ordered **alphabetically by function name** — not by source order. So a file with
`demo_blinky`, `delay`, and `mmio_write` produces MSP430 assembly with sections in
this order:

```
.section .text._RN...rust_begin_unwind   ← panic handler (first alphabetically!)
.section .text.delay
.section .text.demo_blinky               ← our entry point (buried in the middle)
.section .text.mmio_write
```

The COR24 CPU starts executing at address 0x000000. Without intervention, it would
execute the panic handler (an infinite loop), not our demo.

### The Solution: Reset Vector Prologue

The `msp430-to-cor24` translator emits an **absolute jump at address 0** that
jumps to the `start` function. It uses `la`+`jmp` instead of `bra` because
`bra` has a ±127 byte offset limit:

```asm
; Reset vector -> start
    la      r0, start            ; 4 bytes at address 0x000000
    jmp     (r0)                 ; 2 bytes at address 0x000004

; --- function: _RN...rust_begin_unwind ---   (panic handler)
_RN...rust_begin_unwind:
    ...                          ; writes "PANIC\n" to UART, then infinite loop

; --- function: demo_blinky ---
demo_blinky:
    la      r0, 0xFF0000
    lc      r1, 1
    ...

; --- function: start ---                    ; ← CPU jumps here from address 0
start:
    jal     r1, demo_blinky      ; call the demo function
```

This mirrors how real microcontrollers work: the hardware reset vector at address 0
contains a jump to the startup code.

### The `start` Convention

Every COR24 program compiled from Rust uses a fixed entry point convention:

```rust
#[no_mangle]
pub unsafe fn start() -> ! {
    demo_blinky()       // calls the per-program named function
}
```

The `start()` function is the bridge between the CPU reset and the program logic.
Each program has exactly one `start()` that calls its main function.

### How the Entry Point is Identified

1. **Convention (default)**: The translator looks for a `start` label. If the input
   has `.globl` directives (i.e., comes from `rustc`) but no `start` label, it
   returns an error listing the available labels.

2. **Explicit `--entry <func>` flag** (optional override):
   ```bash
   msp430-to-cor24 input.msp430.s --entry my_entry -o output.cor24.s
   ```
   Only needed if a program can't use `start` for some reason.

3. **No `.globl` directives** (e.g., hand-written single-function tests):
   - No prologue emitted — first instruction is at address 0 (legacy behavior)

### In the Rust Source

```rust
#[no_mangle]
pub unsafe fn demo_blinky() -> ! {
    loop {
        mmio_write(LED_ADDR, 1);
        delay(5000);
        mmio_write(LED_ADDR, 0);
        delay(5000);
    }
}

#[no_mangle]
pub unsafe fn start() -> ! {
    demo_blinky()
}
```

The `#[no_mangle]` attribute makes both functions visible as `.globl` symbols in
the MSP430 assembly output. The translator finds `start` and emits the prologue.

## How to Run

### Quick start: run a pre-built demo

```bash
cd rust-to-cor24/demos
bash demo_fibonacci/run.sh
```

This does all three steps automatically:
1. Compiles `src/lib.rs` → `demo_fibonacci.msp430.s` (via `rustc`)
2. Translates → `demo_fibonacci.cor24.s` (via `msp430-to-cor24`)
3. Assembles + runs → dumps registers, memory, UART (via `cor24-run`)

### Run all 13 demos

```bash
cd rust-to-cor24/demos
bash generate-all.sh
```

### Step by step (manual)

```bash
# Build the tools first
cd rust-to-cor24
cargo build --release

# Step 1: Compile Rust → MSP430 assembly
cd demos/demo_fibonacci
rustup run nightly cargo rustc \
    --target msp430-none-elf \
    -Z build-std=core --release \
    -- --emit asm
cp target/msp430-none-elf/release/deps/demo_fibonacci*.s demo_fibonacci.msp430.s

# Step 2: Translate MSP430 → COR24 assembly
../../target/release/msp430-to-cor24 demo_fibonacci.msp430.s \
    -o demo_fibonacci.cor24.s --entry start

# Step 3: Assemble + run in COR24 emulator
../../target/release/cor24-run --run demo_fibonacci.cor24.s --dump
```

### Files produced

After running a demo, the directory contains:
```
demo_fibonacci/
    src/lib.rs                    ← Rust source (you write this)
    demo_fibonacci.msp430.s       ← MSP430 assembly (rustc output)
    demo_fibonacci.cor24.s        ← COR24 assembly (translator output)
    demo_fibonacci.log            ← Emulator output (registers, memory)
```

All intermediate files are human-readable text. There is no binary
object format — `cor24-run` assembles the `.cor24.s` file to bytes
in memory and executes directly.

## Register Mapping

| MSP430 | COR24 | Role |
|--------|-------|------|
| r12 | r0 | arg0 / return value |
| r13 | r1 | arg1 |
| r14 | r2 | arg2 |
| r1 | sp | stack pointer |
| r4-r11 | stack | spilled to fp-relative offsets |

## Calling Convention

The translator uses the `jal` (jump-and-link) calling convention, matching
MakerLisp's COR24 C compiler:

| Aspect | Implementation |
|--------|----------------|
| **Function call** | `la r2, func; jal r1,(r2)` — r1 gets return address |
| **Return** | `jmp (r1)` |
| **Arguments** | In r0 (from MSP430 r12 mapping) |
| **Return value** | In r0 |
| **Caller saves** | `push r1` before `jal`, `pop r1` after |

The `jal` instruction saves the return address in r1 and jumps to the
address in r2, in a single instruction.

## I/O Address Mapping

MSP430 uses 16-bit addresses. COR24 uses 24-bit. The translator maps:

| MSP430 (16-bit) | COR24 (24-bit) | Device |
|------------------|-----------------|--------|
| 0xFF00 | 0xFF0000 | LED D2 / Button S2 |
| 0xFF01 | 0xFF0100 | UART data register |
| 0xFF02 | 0xFF0101 | UART status register |

In Rust source, we define 16-bit constants that the MSP430 target can handle:
```rust
const LED_ADDR: u16 = 0xFF00;     // → 0xFF0000 after translation
const UART_DATA: u16 = 0xFF01;    // → 0xFF0100 after translation
```

## CLI Usage

```bash
# Translate MSP430 .s to COR24 assembly (uses start convention by default)
msp430-to-cor24 input.msp430.s -o output.cor24.s

# Override entry point (rare — only if start can't be used)
msp430-to-cor24 input.msp430.s --entry my_entry -o output.cor24.s

# Compile a Rust project end-to-end
msp430-to-cor24 --compile path/to/rust/project

# Run built-in test case
msp430-to-cor24 --test
```

### Compile mode prerequisites

```bash
rustup toolchain install nightly
rustup target add msp430-none-elf --toolchain nightly
```

## File Types in the Pipeline

| Extension | Format | Description |
|-----------|--------|-------------|
| `.rs` | Rust source | `#![no_std]`, `#[panic_handler]`, `#[no_mangle]` on entry |
| `.msp430.s` | MSP430 asm text | Output of `rustc --emit asm`, `.section .text.<func>` per function |
| `.cor24.s` | COR24 asm text | `la+jmp start` prologue + translated instructions + labels |
| (in-memory) | `Vec<u8>` | Raw bytes from COR24 assembler, loaded at address 0 |

## Project Structure

```
rust-to-cor24/
├── src/
│   ├── lib.rs           # Re-exports translate_msp430
│   ├── msp430.rs        # MSP430 parser + COR24 translator + entry point detection
│   ├── msp430_cli.rs    # CLI: --entry, -o, --compile, --test
│   ├── run.rs           # COR24 emulator runner (assembles + executes)
│   └── pipeline.rs      # WASM pipeline (legacy, unused)
├── output/              # Pre-generated demo outputs
│   ├── demo_blinky.msp430.s   # MSP430 asm from rustc
│   ├── demo_blinky.cor24.s    # COR24 asm with bra prologue
│   └── demo_blinky.log        # Emulator run output
└── data/
    └── pipeline/
        └── demos.rs     # Complete Rust source for all demos
```

## Conventions

1. **`start` entry point**: Every program has `#[no_mangle] pub unsafe fn start()` that calls the main function
2. **`#[no_mangle]`**: Required on `start`, entry functions, and all functions called across translation units
3. **`#[inline(never)]`**: Required on helper functions to prevent inlining (which would eliminate the callable function)
4. **`#![no_std]` + `#[panic_handler]`**: Required — no standard library on bare metal
5. **Panic handler**: Writes "PANIC\n" to UART before entering infinite loop
6. **Reset vector**: `la r0, start` + `jmp (r0)` at address 0 — the COR24 equivalent of a hardware reset vector
7. **Halt convention**: `loop {}` in Rust compiles to a self-branch (`bra .`), detected by the emulator as halt

# /mw-cp — Mike Wright's Checkpoint Process

You are executing a disciplined checkpoint commit. Follow every phase in order.
Do NOT skip any phase, even if it seems clean. Report status after each phase.

---

## Phase 1 — Pre-Commit Checks

Run all of the following that apply to this project. Stop and report failures before proceeding.

1. **Format**: `cargo fmt --check` (or `rustfmt --check` on specific files)
2. **Lint**: `cargo clippy -- -D warnings`
3. **Build**: `cargo build` (debug is fine; surface any compile errors)
4. **Tests**: `cargo test` — all tests must pass
5. **Unused deps** (if `cargo-machete` present): `cargo machete`
6. **Project-local checks**: run `just check` or `just pre-commit` if a justfile exists

If any check fails, STOP. Report what failed and ask the user how to proceed.

---

## Phase 2 — Documentation Updates

Review recent changes and update the following as appropriate:

1. **CHANGELOG.md / CHANGES.md** — add an entry under `[Unreleased]` or today's date
2. **STATUS.md** — update current state, what changed, what's in progress
3. **LEARNINGS.md** — capture anything notable: decisions made, gotchas, tradeoffs
4. **Inline docs** — ensure any new public API items have doc comments (`///`)
5. **README.md** — update if any user-facing behavior, config, or setup changed

If none of these files exist, note that and skip gracefully.

---

## Phase 3 — Staged Diff Review

Run `git diff --staged` (or `git diff HEAD` if nothing staged yet).

Summarize what is changing:
- Files modified, added, deleted
- High-level functional description of the change

Then stage all changes: `git add -A`

---

## Phase 4 — Commit

Compose a detailed commit message following Conventional Commits style:
```
<type>(<scope>): <short imperative summary>

<body — explain WHAT changed and WHY, not HOW>
<mention any tradeoffs, alternatives considered, or follow-ups needed>

Refs: <issue/ticket numbers if applicable>
Co-authored-by: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>
```

Types: `feat`, `fix`, `refactor`, `docs`, `test`, `chore`, `perf`, `build`

If  was provided, incorporate it as context for the message.

Execute: `git commit -m "<message>"`

---

## Phase 5 — Push

1. Check current branch: `git branch --show-current`
2. Check remote: `git remote -v`
3. Push: `git push origin <branch>`

If the push is rejected (non-fast-forward), STOP and report — do NOT force push without explicit user confirmation.

---

## Phase 6 — Checkpoint Summary

Report a brief summary:
- ✅ Checks passed
- 📝 Docs updated (list files)
- 🔖 Commit hash and message
- 🚀 Pushed to remote/branch

Flag any items that were skipped or need follow-up.

Summary of changes:
- **Deleted**: All non-pipeline code (emulator, assembler, ISA, web UI, CLI, components, tests, docs, pages, images, styles, work)
- **Moved**: `rust-to-cor24/` contents to repo root (src/, demos/, examples/, output/, scripts/, docs/)
- **Modified**: Cargo.toml (renamed package, updated deps), source files (updated imports from `cor24_emulator` to `cor24_assembler`/`cor24_emulator`), clippy fixes
- **Added**: `scripts/build.sh`, new `README.md`, `CHANGES.md`, `CLAUDE.md`

## Phase 4 — Commit

input.command:
cd ~/github/sw-embed/sw-cor24-rust && git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
refactor(repo): trim sw-cor24-rust to Rust-to-COR24 pipeline scope

Extract rust-to-cor24/ contents to repo root and remove all non-pipeline
code (emulator, assembler, ISA, web UI, CLI tools, components, tests,
docs, pages). This is step 004 of the COR24 ecosystem refactoring saga.

- Rename package from wasm2cor24 to sw-cor24-rust
- Update deps to use sibling repos (cor24-assembler, cor24-emulator)
- Update imports: assembler types from cor24_assembler, emulator from cor24_emulator
- Mark wasm2cor24 binary as deprecated (MSP430 path preferred)
- Add scripts/build.sh for build verification
- Fix clippy warnings (needless_range_loop, print_literal)
- All 34 tests pass (22 unit + 12 integration)

Co-authored-by: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)"
